**Figure 3.5** Covalent bond formation. In this example, electrons are shared equally between oxygen and hydrogen atoms. This bond is referred to as a polar covalent bond because the oxygen atoms attract the electrons more strongly. As such, the oxygen end of the water molecule has a partial negative charge (δ−) and the hydrogen ends have a partial positive charge (δ+). This polar covalent bond makes water an excellent solvent, as discussed later in this chapter

(Tortora and Derrickson., (2009) / John Wiley & Sons.).

## Definition

A polar covalent bond is a covalent bond in which electrons are shared between atoms but unequally, because one atom attracts the shared electrons more strongly than the other. In the water molecule, the bond between oxygen and hydrogen is described as polar covalent because oxygen attracts the shared electrons more strongly than hydrogen (due to oxygen's greater electronegativity).

## Core Principles

- Covalent bonds form through the sharing of electrons between atoms.
- Covalent bonds can be further classified as non-polar or polar, depending on whether the electrons are shared equally or unequally.
- In non-polar covalent bonds, atoms share the electrons equally, meaning neither atom attracts the shared electrons more than the other. When two or more atoms of the same element form a covalent bond, it is always non-polar.
- When the atoms involved differ in their attraction for the shared electrons, the bond becomes polar covalent rather than purely covalent.
- The atom's power to attract shared electrons to itself is described by its electronegativity; differences in electronegativity between bonded atoms drive bond polarity.
- In water, oxygen attracts the shared electrons more strongly than hydrogen (oxygen has greater electronegativity than hydrogen), producing an unequal distribution of electron density.
- This unequal sharing creates a partial negative charge (δ−) at the oxygen end of the molecule and partial positive charges (δ+) at the hydrogen ends.

## Sub-concepts

- Non-polar covalent bonds (equal electron sharing, e.g., between atoms of the same element).
- Polar covalent bonds (unequal electron sharing).
- Electronegativity as the property determining an atom's attraction for shared electrons.
- Partial charges (δ− and δ+) arising from unequal electron sharing.
- Molecular polarity of water as a consequence of polar covalent O–H bonds.

## Practical Applications

- The polar covalent character of the O–H bond underlies water's role as an excellent solvent, which is central to biochemical reactions and physiological processes discussed later in the chapter.
